all_items: ['Frequent Urination (Polyuria): High glucose levels lead to excessive urination.', 'Excessive Thirst (Polydipsia): Constant thirst from dehydration.', 'Constant Hunger: Feeling hungrier than usual, even after eating.', 'Extreme Fatigue: Feeling very tired or lacking energy.', 'Blurred Vision: High sugar levels can affect the eyes.', 'Slow-Healing Wounds/Sores: Cuts, scrapes, or bruises heal slowly.', 'Tingling or Numbness: Numbness in hands or feet (neuropathy).', 'Frequent Infections: Regular skin, gum, or vaginal yeast infections.', 'Skin Changes: Darkened skin areas, particularly in the neck or armpits.', 'Unexplained Weight Loss: Losing weight without trying.', 'Slow Onset: Symptoms may take years to become noticeable.', 'Asymptomatic: Many people have no symptoms at first and are diagnosed through routine checks.', 'Complications: Left untreated, it can cause severe damage to the heart, eyes, kidneys, and nerves.']

query: what are the symptoms of hypertension
all_items: ['Severe Headaches: Frequently described as throbbing.', 'Vision Changes: Blurry or double vision.', 'Chest Pain: Pressure, pain, or tightness.', 'Shortness of Breath: Difficulty breathing.', 'Neurological Changes: Confusion, numbness, weakness, or difficulty speaking.', 'Other Symptoms: Nosebleeds, severe fatigue, dizziness, or nausea.', 'No Symptoms: Do not wait for symptoms to check your blood pressure.', 'Regular Screening: The only way to know if you have hypertension is through regular, routine checks.', 'Risk Factors: High blood pressure often results from poor diet (excess salt), lack of exercise, smoking, and genetics.']

all_items: ['No Early Warning Signs: High cholesterol does not directly cause symptoms until it has caused significant damage, such as narrowing or blocking arteries.', 'Emergency Symptoms: If high cholesterol has resulted in severe artery blockage (heart disease), you may experience symptoms like chest pain (angina), nausea, extreme fatigue, or shortness of breath.', 'Physical Indicators (Rare): Very high, often genetic, cholesterol levels can cause visible signs like yellow, fatty deposits on the skin (xanthomas) or a grayish-white ring around the cornea of the eye.', 'When to Get Tested: General recommendations suggest adults have their cholesterol checked every 4–6 years, or more frequently if they have risk factors like diabetes, high blood pressure, or a family history of heart disease.']

all_items: ['Fatigue & Lethargy: Constant tiredness is often the first sign.', 'Weight Gain: Unexplained, slow weight gain despite no changes in diet.', 'Cold Intolerance: Feeling much colder than others, even in warm environments.', 'Dry Skin & Hair: Skin may become dry and coarse; hair may become brittle or thin.', 'Mental Changes: Depression, mood changes, and "brain fog" (memory issues, trouble concentrating).', 'Musculoskeletal Pain: Aches, stiffness, and weakness in muscles and joints.', 'Voice/Throat Changes: A hoarse or croaky voice.', 'Digestive Issues: Constipation.', 'Menstrual Changes: Heavier, more frequent, or irregular periods.', 'Physical Changes: Puffy face, droopy eyelids, or swelling in legs.', 'Heart Rate: Slower than normal heart rate (bradycardia).', 'Nerve Issues: Tingling or numbness in the hands (carpal tunnel syndrome).']

query: what are the symptoms of anemia
all_items: ['Fatigue and Weakness: Extreme tiredness is the most common symptom.', 'Pallor: Pale or yellowish skin, which may be more noticeable on the face, under the eyelids, or on the gums.', 'Shortness of Breath (Dyspnea): Breathlessness, especially with physical exertion.', 'Dizziness or Lightheadedness: Feeling faint or lightheaded.', 'Heart Palpitations: A fast, fluttering, or irregular heartbeat.', 'Cold Hands and Feet: Reduced circulation can make extremities feel cold.', 'Headaches: Frequent or persistent headaches.', 'Chest Pain: Especially when the anemia is severe or if pre-existing heart issues exist.', 'Pica: Unusual cravings for non-food items, such as ice, dirt, or paper.', 'Brittle or Spoon-Shaped Nails: Nails that break easily or curve inwards (koilonychia).', 'Sore or Smooth Tongue: A painful, swollen, or unusually smooth tongue.', 'Restless Legs Syndrome: An irresistible urge to move the legs.', 'Hair Loss: Noticing more hair falling out when washing or brushing.']

all_items: ['Fatigue and Tiredness: Persistent exhaustion that does not improve with rest.', 'Bone Pain: Often felt in the lower back, hips, pelvis, ribs, or legs.', 'Muscle Weakness/Aches: Reduced strength, cramping, or difficulty with tasks like climbing stairs.', 'Mood Changes: Feelings of sadness, irritability, or depression.', 'Frequent Infections: Getting sick more easily, including colds and viruses.', 'Hair Loss: Significant or unexpected hair thinning.', 'Slow Wound Healing: Infections or wounds taking longer to heal.', 'Bone Loss: Lowered bone density, increasing the risk of fractures or osteoporosis.', 'Severe Bone Pain/Deformity: Osteomalacia in adults or rickets in children (bowed legs).', 'Muscle Spasms: Cramps, twitches, or tremors (tetany).', 'At Risk: Individuals with limited sun exposure, older adults, those with obesity, and people with darker skin.', 'Management: Often addressed through dietary changes, increased sun exposure, or supplements, generally aiming for 1,500–2,000 IU daily.']

all_items: ['Painful urination: A burning or stinging feeling (dysuria).', 'Frequent, intense urge: A need to pee immediately, even if little comes out.', 'Cloudy or discolored urine: Urine that looks milky, cloudy, or reddish (indicating blood).', 'Strong-smelling urine: A foul odor.', 'Pelvic pain: Specifically in the center of the pelvis and around the pubic bone, especially in women.', 'General fatigue: Feeling tired, shaky, or weak.', 'Fever and chills.', 'Pain in the back or side (flank pain).', 'Nausea or vomiting.', 'Older adults: May experience confusion, delirium, or severe lethargy instead of typical pain symptoms.', 'Children: May exhibit fever, new bedwetting, or pain around the belly button.']

query: what are the symptoms of strep throat
all_items: ['Rapid onset of sore throat.', 'Painful or difficult swallowing.', 'Red, swollen tonsils often with white patches or streaks of pus.', 'Fever (typically $100.4^{\\circ}\\text{F}$ or higher).', 'Small red spots (petechiae) on the roof of the mouth.', 'Swollen, tender lymph nodes in the front of the neck.', 'Headache or general body aches.', 'Nausea, vomiting, or stomach pain (common in children).', 'Scarlatiniform rash (sandpaper-like rash).', 'Stomach ache or vomiting.', 'Persistent fever and sore throat.', 'Irritability or reduced appetite.']

all_items: ['Loud Snoring: Often, but not always, present; generally louder and more chronic than normal snoring.', 'Witnessed Breathing Pauses: A partner often notices the person stops breathing or gasps for air during sleep.', 'Excessive Daytime Sleepiness: Feeling tired, sluggish, or falling asleep unintentionally during the day (hypersomnia).', 'Morning Headaches: Waking up with a headache, often due to low oxygen or high carbon dioxide levels during the night.', 'Dry Mouth or Sore Throat: Waking up with a very dry mouth or throat.', 'Insomnia/Restless Sleep: Difficulty staying asleep or waking up frequently.', 'Mood Changes: Irritability, depression, or lack of focus.', 'Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA): The most common type, caused by throat muscles relaxing too much, blocking the airway.', 'Central Sleep Apnea: Occurs when the brain fails to send proper signals to muscles that control breathing.', 'Risk Factors: Obesity, smoking, alcohol use, age, and anatomical issues in the nose, throat, or mouth.']

all_items: ['Heartburn: A burning pain in the center of the chest, often behind the breastbone.', 'Regurgitation: Acid, food, or liquid backing up into the throat or mouth, causing a sour or bitter taste.', 'Chest Pain: Non-cardiac chest pain, often mistaken for heart issues.', 'Throat and Mouth Issues: Sore throat, hoarseness, chronic cough, or the sensation of a lump in the throat (globus sensation).', 'Digestive Discomfort: Bloating, burping, nausea, or a feeling of fullness.', 'Sleep Disruption: Symptoms often worsen at night when lying flat.', 'Severe or crushing chest pain.', 'Vomiting blood or material that looks like coffee grounds.', 'Black, tarry stools.', 'Sudden weakness, dizziness, or confusion.', 'Foods: Spicy foods, fatty/fried foods, caffeine, chocolate, and citrus.', 'Habits: Eating large meals, lying down immediately after eating, smoking, and excessive alcohol intake.', 'Physical Factors: Being overweight or pregnancy.']

all_items: ['Large Waist Circumference (Abdominal Obesity): $\\ge $ 40 inches (102 cm) for men, $\\ge $ 35 inches (89 cm) for women.', 'High Triglycerides: $\\ge $ 150 mg/dL or higher.', 'Low HDL ("Good") Cholesterol: Less than 40 mg/dL in men or less than 50 mg/dL in women.', 'High Blood Pressure: $\\ge $ 130/80 mm Hg or taking medication for high blood pressure.', 'High Fasting Blood Sugar: $\\ge $ 100 mg/dL or taking medication for high blood sugar.', 'Insulin Resistance: The body cannot use insulin effectively, leading to high blood sugar.', 'Obesity/Overweight: Specifically, carrying extra weight around the waist (apple-shaped body).', 'Physical Inactivity: A sedentary lifestyle.', 'Age and Genetics: Risk increases with age.', 'Weight loss (aim for 7% to 10% of body weight).', 'Regular physical activity (e.g., 30 minutes of walking daily).', 'Healthy eating (e.g., DASH or Mediterranean diets).', 'Smoking cessation.', 'Medication to manage blood pressure, cholesterol, or blood sugar if needed.']

all_items: ['Blood Sugar Levels: A fasting blood sugar level of 100 to 125 mg/dL indicates prediabetes, while a normal range is 70 to 99 mg/dL.', 'Insulin Resistance: Prediabetes often occurs because the body cannot use insulin properly (insulin resistance), leading to sugar buildup in the bloodstream.', 'Symptoms: There are generally no clear symptoms, leading to many people being unaware they have the condition.', 'Risks: Without intervention, people with prediabetes often develop type 2 diabetes within five years.', 'Reversal: Lifestyle improvements, such as consistent exercise and eating nutritious food, can prevent or delay the progression to type 2 diabetes.', 'Weight: Being overweight or obese is a major factor.', 'Age: Adults 45 or older are at higher risk.', 'Family History: Having a parent, brother, or sister with type 2 diabetes increases risk.', 'Physical Activity: Lack of regular physical activity.', 'Consult a Physician: Speak to a healthcare provider about getting tested.', 'Lifestyle Changes: Increase physical activity and adopt a healthier diet to lower blood sugar levels.', 'Follow-Up Screening: Once diagnosed, it is recommended to be checked for progression to type 2 diabetes every one to two years.']

all_items: ['Definition & Readings: Blood pressure is measured using two numbers: Systolic (when the heart beats) over Diastolic (when the heart rests).', 'The "Silent Killer": Many people have no symptoms until significant damage has occurred.', 'Dangers of Untreated Hypertension: Persistent high blood pressure can cause stroke, cardiovascular disease, heart attack, kidney failure, and vision loss.', 'Causes & Risk Factors: Factors include unhealthy diets (high salt/low fruit intake), lack of physical activity, smoking, alcohol consumption, obesity, and stress.', 'Management: Lifestyle changes—such as reducing salt intake, exercising, and maintaining a healthy weight—are primary methods for lowering blood pressure, often alongside prescribed medication.']

all_items: ["Causes: The most common cause is Hashimoto's disease, an autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks the thyroid. Other causes include iodine deficiency, surgical removal of the thyroid, or issues with the pituitary gland.", 'Symptoms: Because thyroid hormones regulate energy, low levels cause a slow metabolism. Common symptoms include:', 'Diagnosis: It is diagnosed through blood tests, typically by measuring thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and thyroxine (T4) levels.', 'Treatment: The standard treatment is daily synthetic thyroid hormone replacement (usually levothyroxine) to restore normal levels, which is generally a lifelong, manageable condition.']

all_items: ['Post-Exertional Malaise (PEM): Symptoms worsen hours or days after activity and can take weeks to recover from.', 'Profound Fatigue: Exhaustion that is not relieved by sleep or rest.', 'Cognitive Dysfunction: Known as "brain fog," this includes memory loss, difficulty concentrating, and slow thinking.', 'Sleep Disturbances: Insomnia, sleeping too much, or not feeling rested upon waking.', 'Pain and Other Symptoms: Muscle/joint pain, headaches, tender lymph nodes, sore throat, and dizziness when standing (orthostatic intolerance).', 'Diagnosis: There is no specific diagnostic test; it is diagnosed by ruling out other conditions and evaluating symptoms.', "Impact: It can significantly reduce a person's ability to perform daily tasks, with severe cases causing bedbound or housebound scenarios.", 'Demographics: While it affects all ages, it is most common in adults aged 20 to 40 and affects more women than men.', 'Cause: The exact cause is unknown, but it often develops after a viral infection.', 'Management: Treatment focuses on managing symptoms, particularly pacing activities to avoid PEM.']

all_items: ['Symptoms: Common symptoms include cramping, stomach pain, bloating, gas, and diarrhea or constipation.', 'Types: IBS is categorized into three main types: IBS-C (constipation-predominant), IBS-D (diarrhea-predominant), and IBS-M (mixed habits).', 'Cause: The exact cause is unknown, but it is considered a disorder of gut-brain interaction, meaning the nerves and muscles in the gut are more sensitive or act irregularly.', 'Diagnosis: Doctors typically diagnose IBS based on symptoms and by ruling out other conditions, often through tests for inflammation or allergies.', 'Management: While there is no cure, symptoms can be managed through stress reduction, increased physical activity, dietary changes (such as avoiding high-FODMAP foods), and medications.']

query: what is a normal blood pressure reading
all_items: ['Normal: Less than $120$ systolic AND less than $80$ diastolic mmHg.', 'Elevated: $120$– $129$ systolic AND less than $80$ diastolic mmHg.', 'High Blood Pressure (Stage 1): $130$– $139$ systolic OR $80$– $89$ diastolic mmHg.', 'High Blood Pressure (Stage 2): $140$ or higher systolic OR $90$ or higher diastolic mmHg.', 'Hypertensive Crisis: Higher than $180$ systolic and/or higher than $120$ diastolic mmHg.', 'Systolic (Top number): Measures pressure in arteries when the heart beats.', 'Diastolic (Bottom number): Measures pressure in arteries when the heart rests between beats.', 'Variations: While below $120/80$ is ideal, average blood pressure can rise slightly with age, but healthy targets generally remain below $130/80$ or $120/80$ for most adults.', 'Monitoring: It is recommended to have your blood pressure checked regularly by a healthcare professional.']

all_items: ['Annual Check-ups: Regular visits to a primary care provider (PCP) to assess overall health, blood pressure, and weight.', 'Screening Tests: Tests to detect potential diseases early, such as cholesterol screenings, mammograms, colorectal cancer screenings, and diabetes screenings.', 'Vaccinations/Immunizations: Shots to prevent illnesses like the flu, tetanus, and HPV.', 'Lifestyle Counseling: Advice on diet, exercise, and smoking cessation to prevent chronic conditions.', 'Early Detection: Finds health issues before they become serious or harder to treat.', 'Cost Savings: Reduces long-term healthcare costs by avoiding major medical crises.', 'Better Health Outcomes: Improves quality of life and longevity by addressing risk factors early.']

all_items: ['Unhealthy Diet: A diet high in salt (sodium) causes fluid retention, increasing blood pressure, while low potassium intake contributes to the problem.', 'Lack of Physical Activity: A sedentary lifestyle can lead to higher heart rates and weight gain, both of which increase pressure.', 'Obesity: Carrying excess weight increases the workload on the heart and affects blood flow, making obesity a key factor.', 'Smoking and Tobacco Use: Tobacco immediately raises blood pressure and damages artery walls.', 'Excessive Alcohol: Regularly drinking too much alcohol can dramatically raise blood pressure.', 'Chronic Conditions: Diseases like diabetes, kidney disease, and sleep apnea can cause hypertension.', 'Age and Genetics: Risk increases with age (particularly over 55) and if family members have the condition.', 'Stress: High levels of chronic stress can contribute to higher blood pressure, often exacerbated by stress-related habits like poor diet.', 'Kidney problems', 'Thyroid issues', 'Sleep apnea', 'Certain medications (e.g., birth control pills, cold remedies, OTC pain relievers)']

all_items: ['Insulin Resistance: Muscles, fat, and liver cells do not respond properly to insulin, resulting in glucose building up in the blood.', 'Obesity and Weight: Being overweight, particularly with a large waist circumference (abdominal fat), is a major cause.', 'Lack of Physical Activity: Sedentary lifestyles limit how the body uses energy, contributing to insulin resistance.', 'Genetics and Family History: Genetic factors play a significant role in susceptibility.', 'Age: Risk increases as people age, particularly over age 35, though it is rising in younger populations.', 'Diet: High consumption of sugary drinks, refined carbohydrates, and red/processed meats.', 'Other Factors: Including prediabetes, smoking, and hormonal imbalances (like Cushing syndrome).']

all_items: ['Unhealthy Diet: Consuming high amounts of saturated fats (red meat, full-fat dairy) and trans fats (packaged snacks, fried foods) raises "bad" (LDL) cholesterol.', 'Lack of Physical Activity: Inactivity reduces "good" (HDL) cholesterol, which helps remove LDL from the blood.', 'Obesity: A Body Mass Index (BMI) of 30 or higher increases your risk.', 'Smoking: Tobacco use damages blood vessel walls, making them more likely to accumulate fatty deposits, and lowers HDL cholesterol.', 'Genetics: Inherited conditions, such as familial hypercholesterolemia (FH), can cause very high LDL levels regardless of lifestyle.', 'Age and Sex: As you get older, your liver becomes less able to remove LDL cholesterol. Risk increases for women after menopause.', 'Medical Conditions: Diabetes, type 2 diabetes, kidney disease, HIV, and thyroid issues can raise cholesterol.', 'Medications: Certain drugs, including some used for blood pressure or HIV, can raise LDL.', 'Diet: Limit saturated fats and focus on fiber-rich foods, vegetables, and unsaturated oils.', 'Exercise: Regular activity can help boost HDL cholesterol.', 'Weight Management: Losing even a few pounds can improve cholesterol levels.', 'Smoking Cessation: Quitting smoking helps increase HDL.']

all_items: ['Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs): The most common cause, causing irritation and a strong urge to pee.', 'Diabetes (Type 1 & 2): High blood sugar causes the body to produce more urine to eliminate excess sugar.', 'Enlarged Prostate (Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia): Common in men over 50, it puts pressure on the urethra.', 'Overactive Bladder Syndrome (OAB): Involuntary bladder contractions create a sudden need to pee.', 'Pregnancy: Hormonal changes and pressure from the fetus on the bladder.', 'Medications: Diuretics (used for high blood pressure) increase urine production.', "Neurological Conditions: Stroke, Multiple Sclerosis, or Parkinson's disease can damage nerves that control the bladder.", 'Fluid Intake: High consumption of water or fluids.', 'Irritants: Caffeine and alcohol act as diuretics and bladder irritants.', 'Stress/Anxiety: Can lead to a psychological need to urinate more frequently.', 'Cold Weather: Known as "cold diuresis," the body increases urine production to keep core temperature stable.', 'Interstitial Cystitis: Chronic bladder inflammation.', 'Kidney Stones or Bladder Stones: Can cause blockage or irritation.', 'Tumors: Bladder or pelvic tumors.']

query: how to treat high blood pressure
all_items: ['Diet: Consume a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy, such as the DASH diet or Mediterranean diet.', 'Sodium Reduction: Reduce salt intake to lower blood pressure, aiming for less than 2,300 mg daily.', 'Physical Activity: Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week, such as brisk walking, cycling, or swimming.', 'Weight Loss: Maintaining a healthy weight reduces strain on the heart.', 'Quit Smoking: Avoid tobacco products, which significantly increase the risk of complications.', 'Stress Management: Practice relaxation techniques such as yoga, meditation, or deep breathing.', 'Diuretics: Often the first medication used, these help the body remove sodium and water.', 'ACE Inhibitors: Relax blood vessels by reducing the production of angiotensin II.', 'ARBs (Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers): Relax blood vessels by blocking the action of angiotensin II.', 'Calcium Channel Blockers: Reduce calcium entering muscle cells to help blood vessels relax.', 'Regular Monitoring: Regularly check blood pressure at home to track the effectiveness of treatment.', 'Medication Adherence: Take medications exactly as prescribed, even if you feel fine.', 'Avoid Triggers: Avoid over-the-counter decongestants as they can increase blood pressure.']

all_items: ['Reduce Saturated Fats: Limit red meat, full-fat dairy, and butter, which raise LDL cholesterol.', 'Increase Soluble Fiber: Consume oats, beans, lentils, Brussels sprouts, and fruits (apples/pears) to block cholesterol absorption.', 'Eat Heart-Healthy Fats: Replace butter with olive oil, rapeseed oil, or avocado.', 'Consume Omega-3 Fatty Acids: Eat oily fish like salmon, mackerel, and herring, or consume walnuts and flaxseeds.', 'Add Whey Protein: Include whey protein in dairy products, which studies show can reduce LDL and total cholesterol.', 'Use Plant Stanols/Sterols: Found in nuts, seeds, and fortified spreads, these help block cholesterol absorption.', 'Exercise Regularly: Aim for 30 minutes of brisk walking, swimming, or cycling 5 days a week to raise HDL ("good") cholesterol.', 'Lose Weight: Shedding even a small amount of weight (e.g., 5-10% of body weight) can improve cholesterol levels.', 'Quit Smoking: Stopping smoking improves HDL levels and benefits heart health.', 'Manage Stress: Chronic stress can increase LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, so use techniques like meditation or exercise.', 'Psyllium: A soluble fiber supplement.', 'Green Tea Extract: Known to help reduce LDL cholesterol.', 'Flaxseed: Rich in fiber and omega-3 fatty acids.']

all_items: ['Main Medication: Levothyroxine (Synthroid, Levo-T).', 'Best Practices: Take daily in the morning on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast.', 'Alternative Medications: While uncommon,liotrix or desiccated thyroid (pig-derived $T_3/T_4$) may be used, though they are less consistent.', 'Time to Feel Better: Most people start feeling better in 1–2 weeks, but full stabilization takes 4–6 weeks.', 'Monitoring: TSH levels are tested 6–8 weeks after starting, and then usually annually.', 'Too High a Dose: Can lead to symptoms of hyperthyroidism (racing heart, insomnia, nervousness).', 'Drug Interactions: Absorption can be affected by iron supplements, calcium supplements, and certain antacids.', 'Lifestyle: A healthy, balanced diet is recommended, though no specific food cures the condition.']

all_items: ['Antacids (Fast Relief): Products like Tums®, Rolaids®, and Mylanta® neutralize stomach acid for quick, short-term relief.', 'H2 Blockers (Longer Relief): Medications like famotidine (Pepcid AC) and cimetidine (Tagamet HB) reduce acid production, offering relief for up to 12 hours.', 'Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs): Stronger medicines that block acid production and allow the esophagus to heal, such as omeprazole (Prilosec) or esomeprazole (Nexium).', 'Alginates: Seaweed-derived compounds like Gaviscon that form a protective foam barrier over stomach contents.', 'Elevate the Bed: Raise the head of your bed 6 to 8 inches to use gravity to keep acid down.', 'Avoid Trigger Foods: Common culprits include spicy foods, fatty foods, chocolate, mint, garlic, onions, citrus fruits, and coffee.', 'Avoid Eating Before Bed: Finish eating 3 or 4 hours before sleeping.', 'Weight Management: Losing excess weight can reduce pressure on the abdomen.', 'Avoid Tight Clothing: Clothes tight around the waist can put pressure on the stomach.', 'Stop Smoking: Smoking can relax the lower esophageal sphincter, allowing acid to escape.']

all_items: ['Hydration: Drink plenty of water (up to 2-3 liters a day) to dilute urine and flush bacteria out of the urinary tract.', 'Heat Therapy: Apply a warm (not hot) heating pad to the lower abdomen or back to reduce pelvic pain and pressure.', 'Over-the-Counter Pain Relievers: Medications such as Acetaminophen (Tylenol), Ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin), or Phenazopyridine (AZO) can reduce pain, fever, and burning sensations.', 'Avoid Irritants: Avoid caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, and carbonated beverages, which can irritate the bladder.', 'Natural Aids: Some studies suggest that drinking 100% cranberry juice or taking supplements like D-mannose can prevent bacteria from sticking to the bladder wall.', 'Fever and chills.', 'Back or side (flank) pain.', 'Nausea or vomiting.', 'Symptoms that last longer than 48 hours or worsen.', 'Urinate Frequently: Do not hold urine, and try to urinate shortly after sexual intercourse.', 'Proper Hygiene: Wipe from front to back after using the restroom.', 'Probiotics: Incorporate foods like yogurt or supplements to help maintain a healthy balance of bacteria.', 'Avoid Irritating Products: Avoid using douches, feminine hygiene sprays, and scented products.']

query: how to lower blood sugar naturally
all_items: ['Exercise Efficiently: Light physical activity like walking or cleaning can lower blood sugar quickly. Consistent exercise increases insulin sensitivity, allowing muscles to use glucose efficiently.', 'Watch Carb Intake: Opt for complex carbs (whole grains, lentils) instead of simple sugars. Portion control is crucial to prevent sugar spikes.', 'Increase Fiber Intake: Soluble fiber (found in vegetables, fruits, and beans) slows carbohydrate digestion and sugar absorption.', 'Stay Hydrated: Drinking water helps kidneys flush out excess sugar, preventing high blood sugar levels.', 'Manage Stress and Sleep: High stress causes hormones to raise blood sugar. Yoga, meditation, and 7–8 hours of sleep improve sugar regulation.', 'Add Nutritional Boosters: Foods rich in chromium and magnesium, along with 1-2 tablespoons of apple cider vinegar, may help regulate sugar levels.', 'Eat meals at regular times.', 'Prioritize protein and healthy fats before carbohydrates.', 'Avoid sugary drinks and snacks.', 'Maintain a healthy weight, which improves insulin sensitivity.']

all_items: ['Antibiotics: Penicillin or amoxicillin are the standard treatments for 10 days.', 'Alternatives: If allergic to penicillin, other options include cephalexin, cefadroxil, or azithromycin.', 'Antibiotic Shot: A single intramuscular injection of penicillin G can sometimes be used.', 'Symptom Management: Over-the-counter medication, such as acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil), relieves throat pain and fever.', 'When to see a doctor: If symptoms do not improve within 48 hours of starting antibiotics, or if you experience high fever, difficulty breathing/swallowing, or a rash.', 'Hydration: Drink plenty of fluids to keep the throat moist.', 'Soothing Foods: Eat soft, cold foods (yogurt, ice cream) or warm liquids (tea, broth).', 'Gargling: Mix $\\frac{1}{4}$ teaspoon of salt in 8 ounces of warm water to gargle, 1-2 times daily.', 'Humidity: Use a cool-mist humidifier to prevent the throat from drying out.', 'Rest: Get plenty of sleep to help the body fight the infection.', 'Prevention: Wash hands frequently with soap and water, do not share eating utensils, and wash, cover your mouth when sneezing/coughing, and keep the environment clean.', 'Contagious Period: A person is typically no longer contagious after 24 hours of taking antibiotics.', 'Feeling Better: Most people feel better 1–2 days after starting antibiotics.', 'Avoiding Complications: Finishing all 10 days of medication is essential to prevent serious, long-term health issues like kidney inflammation or rheumatic fever.']

all_items: ['Iron Deficiency Anemia', 'Vitamin Deficiency Anemia', 'Anemia of Chronic Disease: Focuses on treating the underlying, underlying illness (like cancer or kidney disease) rather than just the blood count.', 'Aplastic Anemia', 'Hemolytic Anemia: May require medication (such as immunosuppressants) or treatment for infection.', 'Blood Transfusion: Used to rapidly replace red blood cells when anemia is severe.', 'Erythropoietin Injections: These shots help the body produce more red blood cells.', 'Bone Marrow Transplant: An option if the bone marrow is unable to produce healthy blood cells.', 'Surgery: Necessary if the anemia is caused by internal bleeding.', 'Duration: It may take several months or longer to build up iron levels.', 'Side Effects: Iron supplements can cause constipation.', 'Medical Care: Always consult a healthcare professional to identify the specific type of anemia for proper, targeted treatment.', "Mayo Clinic's comprehensive guide on Anemia treatment", "Cleveland Clinic's article on Anemia symptoms, causes, and treatment", "American Society of Hematology's patient education on Anemia"]

all_items: ["Ages 19–59: All adults, especially those who haven't been vaccinated before.", 'Age 60 and older: Adults with risk factors (e.g., chronic liver disease, HIV, diabetes) or those who simply want protection.', 'High-Risk Individuals: Healthcare workers, people with multiple sexual partners, people who inject drugs, and travelers to high-risk areas.', 'Heplisav-B: 2-dose series, given 1 month apart (adults 18+).', 'Engerix-B / Recombivax HB: 3-dose series, typically given over 6 months.', 'Twinrix: A combination vaccine for Hepatitis A and B.', 'Effectiveness: 95% effective at preventing infection.', 'Side Effects: Generally safe, with potential for mild side effects like soreness at the injection site.', 'No Booster Needed: Most people do not need a booster dose after completing the series.', 'Pregnancy: The vaccine is safe for pregnant individuals.']
